exercises.name: Explain Like I'm 12
exercises.why: Forces concrete language; surfaces hidden jargon.
exercises.objective: Train the user to explain ideas using only language the listener already has.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: any word a 12-year-old wouldn't recognize counts as jargon. Clarity score MUST reflect jargon density.
exercises.coach_insight: Great reps sound like you're telling a story to a smart kid — short words, familiar objects, one step at a time. The trap: swapping one big word for another big word and calling it simple.
common_failure_modes: Swapping one technical term for another the listener still wouldn't know
Opening with a textbook definition instead of a familiar object or scene
Simplifying the words but keeping sentences long and layered
Going so simple the explanation becomes inaccurate or empty
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the simple words still add up to a correct, complete explanation — simplicity that loses the actual mechanism counts against the rep.
exercises.retry_objective: If jargon slipped in, retry with every technical term replaced by a plain-language equivalent.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names one concrete topic to explain and fixes the listener as a curious 12-year-old with zero background knowledge. The topic varies freely across domains; the vocabulary ceiling and the plain-language training objective never change.

prompts.text: Explain how OAuth works.
Explain what compound interest is.
Explain what mRNA vaccines do.
Explain how a mortgage actually works.
Explain what an API is.
Explain how the stock market sets a company's price.
Explain how machine learning models actually learn.
Explain what insurance companies do behind the scenes.
Explain how encryption keeps your messages private.
Explain why antibiotics stop working over time.
Explain how a recession happens.
Explain how a quantum computer is different from a regular one.
Explain how anesthesia works during surgery.
Explain what a venture capital firm actually does.
Explain how the electoral college decides a president.
Explain why your phone battery dies faster as it ages.

exercises.name: Headline First
exercises.why: Builds the muscle to lead with the point, not the warmup.
exercises.objective: Train the user to deliver the complete answer in the first sentence, before any context or buildup.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if sentence 1 does not carry the thesis, clarity feedback MUST call it out and the clarity score MUST reflect a failure to lead with the point.
exercises.coach_insight: Deliver the verdict as if it's the only sentence you get — 'Yes, ship Friday.' Support comes after. The trap is the warm-up lap: context, caveats, and 'it depends' before the answer lands.
common_failure_modes: Opening with background or context before stating the answer
Sentence one hedges ('it depends') with the real verdict buried later
Restating the question instead of answering it
Leading with a headline, then softening or contradicting it in the support
exercises.scoring_emphasis: After sentence one, weight whether everything that follows actually supports the stated headline rather than drifting into new territory or quietly walking it back.
exercises.retry_objective: If the answer arrived late, retry by stating the full conclusion in sentence one and using everything after it only as support.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt is a direct decision or verdict question from a stakeholder who needs the answer now. The topic and stakes vary; the constant is that the question invites a full answer that must land in sentence one.

prompts.text: Should we ship the feature on Friday?
Was last quarter a success?
Did the new onboarding flow work?
Should we hire two engineers or one senior?
Is the candidate the right fit for the role?
Did the marketing campaign pay for itself?
Should the patient start the new medication?
Was the experiment a success?
Should we accept the acquisition offer?
Is the new curriculum improving outcomes?
Should I take the job in Austin?
Did the team meet its OKRs this quarter?
Is the prototype ready to show the client?
Should we delay the launch by two weeks?
Was the surgery a success?
Should the board approve the new compensation plan?

exercises.name: The Analogy Bridge
exercises.rule: Tie the idea to one everyday thing.
exercises.why: Teaches you to make abstract ideas tangible fast.
exercises.objective: Train the user to make an abstract idea instantly tangible through one everyday analogy that carries the full explanation.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the explanation must ride on one everyday analogy strong enough to carry the idea on its own. A missing, decorative, or mixed analogy MUST drag the clarity score.
exercises.coach_insight: Pick one comparison from everyday life and drive the whole explanation through it — the fridge, the post office, the lunch line. The trap: mentioning an analogy once, then abandoning it for a technical walkthrough.
common_failure_modes: Naming an analogy once, then explaining in technical terms anyway
Stacking two or three analogies instead of committing to one
Choosing a comparison the listener may not know (niche hobby, industry example)
Analogy matches the surface but breaks on the mechanism that actually matters
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight how faithfully the chosen analogy maps to the concept's real mechanism — reward a comparison that would survive a follow-up question, not just a catchy image.
exercises.retry_objective: If the analogy was missing or merely decorative, retry with one everyday comparison strong enough to explain the idea by itself.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names one abstract or technical concept to explain to a listener with no domain background. Topics vary widely across fields; the constant objective is a single everyday analogy that carries the entire explanation.

prompts.text: Explain how database indexing works.
Explain how inflation eats away at savings.
Explain how the immune system fights an infection.
Explain how a neural network learns.
Explain what a hedge fund actually does.
Explain why managers do one-on-ones.
Explain how a good headline pulls readers in.
Explain how a startup burns through its funding.
Explain how the body builds muscle.
Explain how cache memory speeds up a computer.
Explain how supply and demand set a price.
Explain what a derivative is in finance.
Explain how a vaccine trains your body.
Explain how a screenplay's three-act structure works.
Explain how compound interest builds wealth.
Explain how a black hole bends light.

exercises.name: No Jargon Allowed
exercises.rule: Every insider term replaced in real time.
exercises.why: Trains you to translate, not parrot, your domain.
exercises.objective: Train the user to translate insider terminology into plain language in real time while speaking.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: every insider term, acronym, or industry phrase must be replaced with plain language in real time. Unreplaced jargon MUST drag the clarity score.
exercises.coach_insight: Translate as you go: the moment an insider term is about to leave your mouth, say what it means in plain words instead. The trap: defining jargon with more jargon, or naming the term as if saying it were explaining it.
common_failure_modes: Defining one insider term with another undefined term
Saying the jargon first, then bolting on a translation afterward
Dropping acronyms without ever unpacking them
Translations so vague they no longer say anything specific
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the quality of each translation, not just its presence — the plain-language swap must preserve the term's actual meaning for the specific listener named in the prompt.
exercises.retry_objective: If insider terms slipped through untranslated, retry with each one swapped for what it actually means in plain language.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt pairs a jargon-heavy topic from a professional domain with a specific non-expert listener (a relative, a new hire, a worried patient). Topic and listener vary; the real-time translation objective stays constant.

prompts.text: Explain Kubernetes to a non-engineer.
Explain what a hedge fund does to a curious teenager.
Explain agile development to a new client.
Explain a stent procedure to a worried patient.
Explain SEO to a small business owner.
Explain EBITDA to a junior salesperson.
Explain how a CRM works to a new hire.
Explain blockchain to an elderly neighbor.
Explain what a pull request is to a marketing teammate.
Explain what a clinical endpoint means to a patient.
Explain technical debt to a non-technical founder.
Explain how the Federal Reserve moves interest rates to a graduate student.
Explain CRISPR to a high school class.
Explain a P/E ratio to someone new to investing.
Explain UX research methods to a sales team.
Explain herd immunity to a skeptical parent.

exercises.name: Make It Real
exercises.rule: Every claim followed by a concrete detail.
exercises.why: Stops handwaving; grounds abstraction in evidence.
exercises.objective: Train the user to anchor every abstract claim with a concrete detail the listener can picture.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: every claim must be followed by a concrete detail, example, or number in the same breath. Unanchored abstractions MUST drag the clarity score.
exercises.coach_insight: Every time you state a claim, hand the listener something they can picture in the next breath — a number, a scene, a named example. The trap: 'proving' one abstraction with another, like 'it builds trust, which improves culture.'
common_failure_modes: Supporting an abstract claim with another abstraction
Using vague quantifiers ('a lot', 'significantly') where a real number or scenario belongs
Front-loading several claims, then adding one example at the end to cover them all
Details that are vivid but don't actually support the claim they follow
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the pairing rhythm — each individual claim should have its own anchor right beside it, not one example stretched to cover the whole argument.
exercises.retry_objective: If claims floated without grounding, retry by pairing each one with a specific example, number, or scenario the listener can picture.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt is a 'why' question that tempts abstract handwaving, addressed to a listener who needs to be convinced. The topic varies across domains; the constant is that each claim must be grounded in a concrete detail.

prompts.text: Why does diversification reduce risk?
Why is psychological safety important for teams?
Why does sleep affect cognitive performance?
Why do startups fail in their first three years?
Why does code review improve software quality?
Why does early childhood education matter so much?
Why is customer retention more profitable than acquisition?
Why does exercise improve mental health?
Why do interest rate changes affect home prices?
Why is feedback hard to give well?
Why do good story openings hook readers?
Why does monoculture farming hurt the environment?
Why do brand reputations take years to build?
Why does meditation change the brain?
Why do remote teams need more written communication?
Why is it dangerous when regulators get too close to the industries they oversee?

exercises.name: One Point Only
exercises.rule: Pick one idea; everything serves it.
exercises.why: Trains discipline — one clear point beats three muddled ones.
exercises.objective: Train the user to build an entire response around a single central idea and let nothing stray from it.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must serve one central idea from start to finish. Any sentence that introduces a second idea MUST drag the clarity score.
exercises.coach_insight: Choose your one idea before you speak, say it plainly, and treat every sentence after as its bodyguard. The trap is the 'and also' reflex — a second good point that splits the listener's attention and weakens both.
common_failure_modes: Smuggling in a second point as an 'and also' or 'one more thing'
Listing several causes instead of committing to the biggest one
Supporting details that quietly shift the central idea midway through
Never stating the single point explicitly, leaving the listener to infer it
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight commitment — whether the speaker names one idea outright and whether every later sentence demonstrably advances it, not merely avoids contradicting it.
exercises.retry_objective: If a second idea crept in, retry by naming the one central point up front and cutting every sentence that doesn't serve it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt asks for the single reason, risk, or lesson behind a situation, addressed to a listener who needs one takeaway. The scenario varies; the discipline of choosing and serving one central idea never does.

prompts.text: Why did our team's velocity drop this sprint?
What's the single biggest risk to next year's plan?
Why did the customer churn?
Why is this candidate the right hire?
What's the one thing we need to change about the product?
Why should the board approve the budget increase?
What's the most important lesson from last quarter?
Why did the patient's recovery take longer than expected?
What's the single biggest reason readers love this book?
Why did the prototype fail user testing?
What's the one reason this market is worth entering?
Why is our team's morale slipping?
What's the one habit that has improved your life the most?
Why is climate change accelerating faster than predicted?
Why did the merger underperform?
What's the single thing students need to succeed in college?

exercises.name: Answer the Confusion
exercises.rule: Answer the question a confused listener would ask.
exercises.why: Forces you to model the listener, not just deliver.
exercises.objective: Train the user to anticipate where a listener actually gets confused and resolve that confusion head-on.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the response must answer the question a confused listener would actually ask, not just recite facts. Failing to address the likely point of confusion MUST drag the clarity score.
exercises.coach_insight: Start where the listener is stuck — name the mix-up out loud, then untangle it head-on. The trap: reciting two correct definitions side by side without ever touching why people confuse them in the first place.
common_failure_modes: Reciting parallel definitions without addressing why the two get confused
Never naming the misconception the listener actually holds
Explaining the easy half of the pair and skimming the genuinely confusing half
Introducing new concepts that create fresh confusion instead of resolving the original
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the likely misunderstanding is named explicitly and dismantled — a technically accurate answer that ignores the confusion should score below a simpler one that resolves it.
exercises.retry_objective: If the response explained around the confusion instead of resolving it, retry by naming the likely misunderstanding first and answering it directly.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt targets a commonly confused pair of concepts or a popular misconception, with an earnest listener who half-understands it. Topics vary; the constant objective is modeling the listener's confusion and answering the question they would actually ask.

prompts.text: Explain the difference between revenue and profit.
Explain the difference between weather and climate.
Explain why correlation isn't causation.
Explain the difference between a recession and a depression.
Explain the difference between bacteria and viruses.
Explain the difference between a stock and a bond.
Explain the difference between marketing and sales.
Explain the difference between leadership and management.
Explain the difference between RAM and storage.
Explain why interest rates and bond prices move opposite ways.
Explain the difference between empathy and sympathy.
Explain why a generic drug works the same as the brand name.
Explain the difference between a startup and a small business.
Explain why your tax refund isn't free money.
Explain the difference between machine learning and AI.
Explain why a college degree isn't a guarantee of a job.

exercises.name: Prove It
exercises.rule: Every claim proven in the same breath.
exercises.why: Builds the habit of carrying evidence, not just opinion.
exercises.objective: Train the user to attach proof to every claim in the same breath the claim is made.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: every claim must be paired with proof — a reason, number, or specific example — in the same breath. Unsupported assertions MUST drag the clarity score.
exercises.coach_insight: Speak in claim-proof pairs: make the assertion, then immediately give the number, example, or reason a skeptic can't wave away. The trap: stacking three confident claims and saving all the evidence for a finale that never comes.
common_failure_modes: Making several claims in a row before any evidence appears
Backing a claim with the same opinion restated in different words
Offering evidence too generic to check ('studies show', 'everyone knows')
Proof that supports a weaker claim than the one actually made
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the strength of each proof against its claim — evidence a skeptic could dismiss with one question counts barely more than no evidence at all.
exercises.retry_objective: If any claim went out unsupported, retry by following each assertion immediately with one specific piece of evidence.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt is a 'Tell me why...' persuasion ask from a skeptical listener. Topic and stakes vary; the constant is that every claim must arrive with its proof in the same breath.

prompts.text: Tell me why remote work is better.
Tell me why your product is worth the price.
Tell me why the new strategy is the right one.
Tell me why this drug should get FDA approval.
Tell me why your team deserves the bigger budget.
Tell me why this candidate is better than the other finalist.
Tell me why we should rewrite this codebase.
Tell me why early retirement is a realistic goal.
Tell me why this novel deserves the award.
Tell me why nuclear power is part of the climate answer.
Tell me why this startup will be a unicorn.
Tell me why college is still worth it.
Tell me why meditation belongs in your morning.
Tell me why the marketing budget should double next year.
Tell me why this design is the strongest of the three.
Tell me why you'd choose this co-founder.

exercises.name: The Word Budget
exercises.rule: Strict 30-word ceiling. Say everything inside it.
exercises.why: Forces ruthless prioritization at the sentence level.
exercises.objective: Train the user to prioritize ruthlessly enough to land a complete point inside a strict 30-word budget.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the transcript exceeds 30 words, the primary clarity feedback MUST call it out and the conciseness score MUST reflect the budget violation.
exercises.coach_insight: Decide the one indispensable point, spend your words on it first, and stop when it's said — a great rep sounds finished at word 25. The trap: talking normally and just stopping early, leaving the point half-delivered.
common_failure_modes: Running past the 30-word ceiling
Stopping mid-thought at the limit, leaving the point incomplete
Spending the budget on setup or throat-clearing instead of the point itself
Cramming a breathless list of topics without saying anything about them
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight completeness inside the ceiling — the winning rep is judged on whether the point is whole at the final word, not merely on staying under budget.
exercises.retry_objective: If the budget was blown, retry by cutting every non-essential word until the complete point fits inside 30 words.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt asks for one complete brief — a pitch, summary, or update — for a busy, named audience. Topic varies; the 30-word ceiling and the ruthless-prioritization objective are fixed in every generated prompt.

prompts.text: Brief a busy new executive on your team's roadmap.
Pitch your company in an elevator.
Summarize last quarter's results for the board.
Describe your path so far to a busy recruiter.
You're a fund manager. Explain your investment thesis to a busy client.
Sum up the plot of your favorite movie for a friend rushing out the door.
Describe your ideal customer to a new sales hire on day one.
Explain to a busy VP why your team missed its deadline.
Summarize the patient's condition for the next shift.
Tell a busy investor the biggest risk facing your business.
You meet a stranger at an event. Explain what your product does.
Sum up a news story everyone's talking about for a friend who missed it.
Describe your leadership style to an interviewer who's short on time.
Brief a busy lab director on the one thing your latest experiment proved.
Pitch a new course to a curious student.
Brief the CEO on a brewing PR crisis.
